content.assessment: A transparency and evidence infrastructure is the proposal best positioned to correctly frame the demographic questions this lens raises, particularly by explicitly tracking Hungary-specific outcomes rather than assuming international findings (e.g., the contested closure-depopulation link) transfer directly. Its core limitation from this lens is temporal: it establishes machinery for future decisions while the underlying cohort collapse and zero-enrollment cliffs continue unaddressed during the buildout period, and an information framework carries no independent capacity to alter cohort trajectories or spatial distribution.
opportunities: Registry design could formally incorporate a demographic planning horizon (e.g., using cohort projections from current TFR and birth-decline trends) so that decisions anticipate rather than react to the enrollment cliff
strengths: Explicitly commits to tracking Hungary-specific outcomes rather than importing contested international evidence on closure-depopulation links, appropriately treating that evidence as unresolved rather than settled

A registry-based approach could formally incorporate cohort-decline and age-structure divergence data (e.g., the 91 vs. 42 municipality split) into future viability determinations, improving the evidentiary basis for whichever structural model is chosen
threats: Historical pattern of top-down political decisions overriding technical evidence suggests demographic data alone will not determine outcomes, consistent with this lens's own limitation that population projections do not dictate the normative response
Given the pace of cohort contraction (22% over four years), a purely observational framework risks documenting collapse rather than informing timely intervention
weaknesses: Does not itself alter cohort trajectory, spatial distribution, or the zero-enrollment cliff already documented in the smallest settlements
Establishing evidence infrastructure takes time, during which the fastest-declining cohorts continue contracting past the point of any registry-informed intervention
